title: von Willebrand Disease
oneLiner: Inherited bleeding disorder due to reduced or defective von Willebrand factor (vWF) — causes mucocutaneous bleeding and prolonged bleeding after trauma/surgery.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (rare but fatal in type 3):** Sudden severe headache, neurological deficit, vomiting, or fall with head strike. **The single finding:** New focal neurology + coagulopathy (prolonged APTT + low vWF activity) — do not delay CT head.
- **Major haemorrhage after surgery/trauma (especially in undiagnosed VWD):** Unexplained hypotension, falling Hb, rapidly expanding haematoma, or bleeding from multiple sites. **The single finding:** Bleeding continues despite standard surgical haemostasis + normal platelet count — ask team if they checked vWF pre-op.
- **Severe menorrhagia with haemodynamic instability:** Heavy vaginal bleeding leading to syncope/presyncope, tachycardia, low BP. **The single finding:** Bleeding not controlled by tranexamic acid alone + Hb drop >2 g/dL — needs gynae + haematology review for vWF replacement.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Easy bruising (large, non-tender ecchymoses), epistaxis (often bilateral, lasting >20 min), menorrhagia (especially since menarche)
  - Prolonged bleeding after dental extraction, tonsillectomy, or other mucosal procedures
  - Gum bleeding with brushing, GI bleeding (rare, usually with severe deficiency)
  - Post-partum haemorrhage (often delayed, >24h)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No petechiae (helps differentiate from platelet disorders) – unless concurrent thrombocytopenia
  - Large haematomas from minor trauma
  - Joint bleeds: rare except in type 3 (unlike haemophilia)
  - Ecchymoses in unusual sites (back, thighs)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Haemophilia A/B** – joint bleeds common, mucocutaneous bleeding rare; factor VIII/VWF activity normal or low (if vWF low, may be combined?)
  - **Platelet function disorders (e.g., Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann)** – petechiae present, bleeding time prolonged, PFA-100 abnormal; vWF activity normal
  - **Acquired von Willebrand syndrome (AVWS)** – older patient, no family history, underlying hypothyroidism, lymphoproliferative disease, or monoclonal gammopathy
  - **Mild thrombocytopenia (e.g., ITP)** – petechiae, isolated low plt count, normal coagulation
  - **Vitamin C deficiency / scurvy** – perifollicular haemorrhages, joint pain, gingival hyperplasia

- **Splitter:** **Joint bleeding** points to haemophilia; **mucocutaneous bleeding + prolonged closure time (PFA-100)** points to VWD (or platelet disorder). If PFA-100 normal but clinical suspicion remains, order vWF assays.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Bleeding time – no longer recommended (unreliable, painful)
  - PFA-100 (closure time) – if available, normal rules out most VWD except mild type 1; prolonged suggests defect
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, plt count), PT, APTT, fibrinogen
  - **Essential panel:** vWF antigen (vWF:Ag), vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor, vWF:RCo), factor VIII activity, blood group
  - Multimer analysis (for typing, done by specialist haematology lab)
- **Imaging:**
  - **None** unless suspected intracranial bleed (CT head) or retroperitoneal bleed (CT abdomen with contrast)
- **Special tests:**
  - Desmopressin (DDAVP) challenge – performed by haematology nurse specialist; measure vWF:RCo and FVIII before, 1h, 2h, 4h after. Determines responsiveness (type 1 vs 2 vs 3)
  - VWF:CB (collagen binding) – can help differentiate type 2 variants
- **Never-miss test:** **Full coagulation screen + vWF:RCo and vWF:Ag** in any patient with unexplained mucocutaneous bleeding, especially before surgery. Omission can lead to massive post-op haemorrhage.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Active severe bleeding:**
    - Call haematology registrar on call; consider transfer to haemophilia centre if possible
    - **vWF-containing concentrate** (e.g., Wilate, Haemate P) – [check local policy] for dose (typically 40–60 U/kg vWF:RCo IV, aim for >100% activity)
    - **Tranexamic acid** – 1g IV over 10 min, then 1g IV q8h (or 15mg/kg IV loading)
    - **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** – 0.3 mcg/kg IV in 50mL NaCl 0.9% over 30 minutes **only if** known type 1 and responsive (do NOT use in type 2B – risk thrombocytopenia, or type 3 – no response)
  - **Minor bleeding (epistaxis, dental):**
    - Tranexamic acid mouthwash 5% (10ml qid for 2 days) or oral TXA 1g TDS
    - Local measures: pressure, nasal pack, Surgicel
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Monitor for rebleeding:** Check Hb daily, vWF activity/FVIII levels if on concentrate (usually target >50% for 3-7 days)
  - **Desmopressin responsive patients:** May repeat DDAVP at 12-24h interval, but limited to 2-3 doses due to tachyphylaxis and hyponatraemia risk
  - **Fluids:** Monitor fluid balance; DDAVP can cause hyponatraemia – check Na at 12h and 24h post dose. Restrict free water if needed.
  - **Iron status:** If significant blood loss, check ferritin; start oral iron if deficient
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Type 1 (mild):** Tranexamic acid PRN for menorrhagia/epistaxis; desmopressin for surgery/haemorrhage
  - **Type 2:** Avoid DDAVP in 2B; use concentrate for surgery. 2A and 2M may respond to DDAVP – check after trial.
  - **Type 3 (severe):** Prophylactic vWF concentrate 2-3x/week [check local policy], central venous access? Haematology specialist follow-up
  - **Menorrhagia:** Tranexamic acid, combined oral contraceptive, Mirena IUS; if fails → check for concurrent bleeding disorder
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Normal saline 1L/8h (unless DDAVP given – then 1L/24h to avoid hyponatraemia)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** **Avoid** mechanical/chemical prophylaxis if actively bleeding or high risk; if on treatment, use intermittent pneumatic compression only until bleeding controlled. Then reassess with haematology.
  - **Diet:** Normal (no restrictions) – watch for gum bleeding if oral injury
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 hourly for 4h if bleeding, then 4-hourly if stable. If on DDAVP, check Na at 12h.
  - **Avoid aspirin, NSAIDs, clopidogrel, warfarin – absolutely.**
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Desmopressin (DDAVP) IV:** 0.3 mcg/kg in 50-100mL NaCl 0.9% over 30 min. Check response is adequate (vWF:RCo rise >50% and >0.5 IU/mL)
  - **Desmopressin intranasal spray:** 150 mcg per spray (one spray each nostril) – use only if known responsive and minor dental/surgery. (BNF dose: 300 mcg as single dose for haemophilia A/vWD)
  - **Tranexamic acid IV:** 15 mg/kg (or 1g) IV over 10 min, then 1g IV q8h (or oral 1g TDS for 5-7 days)
  - **vWF-containing concentrate:** [check local policy] – dose in units vWF:RCo. Typically 40-60 U/kg for major bleeding; 30 U/kg for minor.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cryoprecipitate – avoid unless vWF concentrate not available (risk of blood-borne infection)
  - Oestrogen-containing contraceptives (for menorrhagia)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Desmopressin:** Avoid in type 2B (risk thrombocytopenia); caution in coronary artery disease (vasospasm); monitor Na in <2 years old, elderly, or those with impaired thirst
  - **Tranexamic acid:** Avoid if gross haematuria (clot retention); caution in DIC; reduce dose if GFR <50 mL/min ([check local policy: 15mg/kg q12h if GFR 30-50])
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Tranexamic acid:** Reduce dose in renal impairment (accumulation risk – fits). GFR 10-30: 1g IV stat, then 500mg IV q12h. GFR <10: 1g IV stat, then 500mg IV q24h.
  - **Desmopressin:** No adjustment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving desmopressin to a patient with type 2B VWD** – can cause severe thrombocytopenia and worsen bleeding. Always check VWD type and response history before prescribing DDAVP.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Severe haemorrhage (retroperitoneal, intra-abdominal, intracranial)
  - Hyponatraemia (DDAVP) – can lead to seizures if Na <125 mmol/L
  - Anaphylaxis to vWF concentrate (rare)
  - Thrombosis (rare, with high-dose concentrate)
- **Chronic:**
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (especially from menorrhagia or recurrent epistaxis)
  - Joint arthropathy (type 3) – similar to haemophilia if recurrent bleeds
  - Psychological impact / anxiety about bleeding
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent for type 1 (mild) – normal life expectancy, menorrhagia manageable. Type 3 (severe) requires lifelong specialist care; with prophylaxis, bleeding risk reduced but joint disease possible. Overall mortality low except from intracranial bleed.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check vWF:RCo and vWF:Ag before any elective surgery** in a patient with undiagnosed bruising – a normal screen avoids disaster.
- **Give tranexamic acid routinely for dental procedures** – 1g oral 1h pre-op and 1g TDS for 2 days reduces bleeding significantly.
- **In menorrhagia, start tranexamic acid 1g TDS during menses** – it is safe and effective; if fails, add tranexamic acid + NSAIDs (but NSAIDs only if platelet function normal – check with haematology).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DO NOT give NSAIDs or aspirin** to any patient with VWD – they impair platelet function and can precipitate major bleeding.
- **DO NOT use desmopressin without confirming type and response** – especially in type 2B (can cause thrombocytopenia) and type 3 (no response).
- **DO NOT discharge a patient with heavy menorrhagia without checking Hb and ferritin** – they can be severely iron deficient and need oral iron therapy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Large spontaneous bruises or haematomas >5cm
  - Prolonged nosebleed (>20 min) not controlled with pressure
  - Black/tarry stools or blood in urine
  - New severe headache, vomiting, or focal neuro symptoms
  - Menstrual bleeding soaking through pad/hour for >2h
- **Follow-up:** Haematology clinic within 2-4 weeks for new diagnosis; annual review in established VWD. For menorrhagia, consider gynaecology referral if failed medical therapy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call haematology reg immediately if:**
  - Active major bleeding (Hb drop >2g/dL, haemodynamic instability)
  - Suspected intracranial bleed (CT head + call neuro team)
  - Need for vWF concentrate or DDAVP (do not give without haematology input)
  - Any new bleeding on treatment (poorly controlled)
- **ICU / crash team:** NEWS2 ≥6, GCS <13, lactate >4, refractory hypotension.

rawMarkdown:
# von Willebrand Disease

**One-liner:** Inherited bleeding disorder due to reduced or defective von Willebrand factor (vWF) — causes mucocutaneous bleeding and prolonged bleeding after trauma/surgery.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Intracranial haemorrhage (rare but fatal in type 3):** Sudden severe headache, neurological deficit, vomiting, or fall with head strike. **The single finding:** New focal neurology + coagulopathy (prolonged APTT + low vWF activity) — do not delay CT head.
- **Major haemorrhage after surgery/trauma (especially in undiagnosed VWD):** Unexplained hypotension, falling Hb, rapidly expanding haematoma, or bleeding from multiple sites. **The single finding:** Bleeding continues despite standard surgical haemostasis + normal platelet count — ask team if they checked vWF pre-op.
- **Severe menorrhagia with haemodynamic instability:** Heavy vaginal bleeding leading to syncope/presyncope, tachycardia, low BP. **The single finding:** Bleeding not controlled by tranexamic acid alone + Hb drop >2 g/dL — needs gynae + haematology review for vWF replacement.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Easy bruising (large, non-tender ecchymoses), epistaxis (often bilateral, lasting >20 min), menorrhagia (especially since menarche)
  - Prolonged bleeding after dental extraction, tonsillectomy, or other mucosal procedures
  - Gum bleeding with brushing, GI bleeding (rare, usually with severe deficiency)
  - Post-partum haemorrhage (often delayed, >24h)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No petechiae (helps differentiate from platelet disorders) – unless concurrent thrombocytopenia
  - Large haematomas from minor trauma
  - Joint bleeds: rare except in type 3 (unlike haemophilia)
  - Ecchymoses in unusual sites (back, thighs)

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Haemophilia A/B** – joint bleeds common, mucocutaneous bleeding rare; factor VIII/VWF activity normal or low (if vWF low, may be combined?)
  - **Platelet function disorders (e.g., Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann)** – petechiae present, bleeding time prolonged, PFA-100 abnormal; vWF activity normal
  - **Acquired von Willebrand syndrome (AVWS)** – older patient, no family history, underlying hypothyroidism, lymphoproliferative disease, or monoclonal gammopathy
  - **Mild thrombocytopenia (e.g., ITP)** – petechiae, isolated low plt count, normal coagulation
  - **Vitamin C deficiency / scurvy** – perifollicular haemorrhages, joint pain, gingival hyperplasia

- **Splitter:** **Joint bleeding** points to haemophilia; **mucocutaneous bleeding + prolonged closure time (PFA-100)** points to VWD (or platelet disorder). If PFA-100 normal but clinical suspicion remains, order vWF assays.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - Bleeding time – no longer recommended (unreliable, painful)
  - PFA-100 (closure time) – if available, normal rules out most VWD except mild type 1; prolonged suggests defect
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, plt count), PT, APTT, fibrinogen
  - **Essential panel:** vWF antigen (vWF:Ag), vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor, vWF:RCo), factor VIII activity, blood group
  - Multimer analysis (for typing, done by specialist haematology lab)
- **Imaging:**
  - **None** unless suspected intracranial bleed (CT head) or retroperitoneal bleed (CT abdomen with contrast)
- **Special tests:**
  - Desmopressin (DDAVP) challenge – performed by haematology nurse specialist; measure vWF:RCo and FVIII before, 1h, 2h, 4h after. Determines responsiveness (type 1 vs 2 vs 3)
  - VWF:CB (collagen binding) – can help differentiate type 2 variants
- **Never-miss test:** **Full coagulation screen + vWF:RCo and vWF:Ag** in any patient with unexplained mucocutaneous bleeding, especially before surgery. Omission can lead to massive post-op haemorrhage.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Active severe bleeding:**
    - Call haematology registrar on call; consider transfer to haemophilia centre if possible
    - **vWF-containing concentrate** (e.g., Wilate, Haemate P) – [check local policy] for dose (typically 40–60 U/kg vWF:RCo IV, aim for >100% activity)
    - **Tranexamic acid** – 1g IV over 10 min, then 1g IV q8h (or 15mg/kg IV loading)
    - **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** – 0.3 mcg/kg IV in 50mL NaCl 0.9% over 30 minutes **only if** known type 1 and responsive (do NOT use in type 2B – risk thrombocytopenia, or type 3 – no response)
  - **Minor bleeding (epistaxis, dental):**
    - Tranexamic acid mouthwash 5% (10ml qid for 2 days) or oral TXA 1g TDS
    - Local measures: pressure, nasal pack, Surgicel
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Monitor for rebleeding:** Check Hb daily, vWF activity/FVIII levels if on concentrate (usually target >50% for 3-7 days)
  - **Desmopressin responsive patients:** May repeat DDAVP at 12-24h interval, but limited to 2-3 doses due to tachyphylaxis and hyponatraemia risk
  - **Fluids:** Monitor fluid balance; DDAVP can cause hyponatraemia – check Na at 12h and 24h post dose. Restrict free water if needed.
  - **Iron status:** If significant blood loss, check ferritin; start oral iron if deficient
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Type 1 (mild):** Tranexamic acid PRN for menorrhagia/epistaxis; desmopressin for surgery/haemorrhage
  - **Type 2:** Avoid DDAVP in 2B; use concentrate for surgery. 2A and 2M may respond to DDAVP – check after trial.
  - **Type 3 (severe):** Prophylactic vWF concentrate 2-3x/week [check local policy], central venous access? Haematology specialist follow-up
  - **Menorrhagia:** Tranexamic acid, combined oral contraceptive, Mirena IUS; if fails → check for concurrent bleeding disorder
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Normal saline 1L/8h (unless DDAVP given – then 1L/24h to avoid hyponatraemia)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** **Avoid** mechanical/chemical prophylaxis if actively bleeding or high risk; if on treatment, use intermittent pneumatic compression only until bleeding controlled. Then reassess with haematology.
  - **Diet:** Normal (no restrictions) – watch for gum bleeding if oral injury
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 hourly for 4h if bleeding, then 4-hourly if stable. If on DDAVP, check Na at 12h.
  - **Avoid aspirin, NSAIDs, clopidogrel, warfarin – absolutely.**

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents:**
  - **Desmopressin (DDAVP) IV:** 0.3 mcg/kg in 50-100mL NaCl 0.9% over 30 min. Check response is adequate (vWF:RCo rise >50% and >0.5 IU/mL)
  - **Desmopressin intranasal spray:** 150 mcg per spray (one spray each nostril) – use only if known responsive and minor dental/surgery. (BNF dose: 300 mcg as single dose for haemophilia A/vWD)
  - **Tranexamic acid IV:** 15 mg/kg (or 1g) IV over 10 min, then 1g IV q8h (or oral 1g TDS for 5-7 days)
  - **vWF-containing concentrate:** [check local policy] – dose in units vWF:RCo. Typically 40-60 U/kg for major bleeding; 30 U/kg for minor.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cryoprecipitate – avoid unless vWF concentrate not available (risk of blood-borne infection)
  - Oestrogen-containing contraceptives (for menorrhagia)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Desmopressin:** Avoid in type 2B (risk thrombocytopenia); caution in coronary artery disease (vasospasm); monitor Na in <2 years old, elderly, or those with impaired thirst
  - **Tranexamic acid:** Avoid if gross haematuria (clot retention); caution in DIC; reduce dose if GFR <50 mL/min ([check local policy: 15mg/kg q12h if GFR 30-50])
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Tranexamic acid:** Reduce dose in renal impairment (accumulation risk – fits). GFR 10-30: 1g IV stat, then 500mg IV q12h. GFR <10: 1g IV stat, then 500mg IV q24h.
  - **Desmopressin:** No adjustment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving desmopressin to a patient with type 2B VWD** – can cause severe thrombocytopenia and worsen bleeding. Always check VWD type and response history before prescribing DDAVP.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Severe haemorrhage (retroperitoneal, intra-abdominal, intracranial)
  - Hyponatraemia (DDAVP) – can lead to seizures if Na <125 mmol/L
  - Anaphylaxis to vWF concentrate (rare)
  - Thrombosis (rare, with high-dose concentrate)
- **Chronic:**
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (especially from menorrhagia or recurrent epistaxis)
  - Joint arthropathy (type 3) – similar to haemophilia if recurrent bleeds
  - Psychological impact / anxiety about bleeding

### Prognosis

- Excellent for type 1 (mild) – normal life expectancy, menorrhagia manageable. Type 3 (severe) requires lifelong specialist care; with prophylaxis, bleeding risk reduced but joint disease possible. Overall mortality low except from intracranial bleed.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always check vWF:RCo and vWF:Ag before any elective surgery** in a patient with undiagnosed bruising – a normal screen avoids disaster.
- **Give tranexamic acid routinely for dental procedures** – 1g oral 1h pre-op and 1g TDS for 2 days reduces bleeding significantly.
- **In menorrhagia, start tranexamic acid 1g TDS during menses** – it is safe and effective; if fails, add tranexamic acid + NSAIDs (but NSAIDs only if platelet function normal – check with haematology).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **DO NOT give NSAIDs or aspirin** to any patient with VWD – they impair platelet function and can precipitate major bleeding.
- **DO NOT use desmopressin without confirming type and response** – especially in type 2B (can cause thrombocytopenia) and type 3 (no response).
- **DO NOT discharge a patient with heavy menorrhagia without checking Hb and ferritin** – they can be severely iron deficient and need oral iron therapy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - Large spontaneous bruises or haematomas >5cm
  - Prolonged nosebleed (>20 min) not controlled with pressure
  - Black/tarry stools or blood in urine
  - New severe headache, vomiting, or focal neuro symptoms
  - Menstrual bleeding soaking through pad/hour for >2h
- **Follow-up:** Haematology clinic within 2-4 weeks for new diagnosis; annual review in established VWD. For menorrhagia, consider gynaecology referral if failed medical therapy.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call haematology reg immediately if:**
  - Active major bleeding (Hb drop >2g/dL, haemodynamic instability)
  - Suspected intracranial bleed (CT head + call neuro team)
  - Need for vWF concentrate or DDAVP (do not give without haematology input)
  - Any new bleeding on treatment (poorly controlled)
- **ICU / crash team:** NEWS2 ≥6, GCS <13, lactate >4, refractory hypotension.
